positioning: Productized daily MEP BIM education for beginners and early-stage BIM learners.

lessons.topic: What MEP BIM Coordination Really Means
lessons.learning_outcome: Open any MEP model or sample drawing and choose one crowded ceiling area. Write down three things that could become a coordination issue even if there is no hard clash.

lessons.topic: Why System Names Matter in Revit MEP
lessons.learning_outcome: Pick five MEP elements in a sample model. Record category, system name, size, and level for each one.

lessons.topic: Clearance Is Not the Same as Clash
lessons.learning_outcome: Choose one pipe, duct, or cable tray route. Describe the extra space needed around it for installation and maintenance.
Take one MEP drawing sheet and identify three pieces of information that cannot be fully understood from plan view alone.
Create a three-column checklist: Geometry, Data, Coordination. Add three checks under each column.

lessons.topic: Supply Air, Return Air, Exhaust Air, and Outdoor Air
lessons.learning_outcome: List four air systems and write one coordination concern for each.
Sketch one simple supply-return loop and mark equipment, valves, and flow direction.
Choose one tray route and identify where a bend, drop, or access issue could happen.

lessons.topic: Sprinkler Coordination Is More Than Head Layout
lessons.learning_outcome: Take a small ceiling area and list every ceiling device that must coordinate with sprinkler heads.
Pick one drainage route and calculate whether the end elevation will be lower than the start elevation.

lessons.topic: Levels, Views, and Why They Affect MEP Modeling
lessons.learning_outcome: Select one MEP element and record its level, offset, and visibility in plan, section, and 3D.
Select one MEP family and identify three type parameters and three instance parameters.

lessons.topic: Using Sections to Understand Congested Areas
lessons.learning_outcome: Create or imagine one section through a crowded ceiling zone and list the systems from top to bottom.
Look at a sample clash list and create three groups: critical, repeated, and low-priority.
Write a 10-line personal MEP BIM checklist based on Days 1-14.
Choose one element category and list five parameters that would matter for coordination or reporting.
Create a sample schedule idea for one category and decide which columns would help QA.

lessons.topic: Why Coordinates Matter Before Clash Review
lessons.learning_outcome: Write down three alignment checks you would perform before a coordination review.
List the workset types you would expect in a simple MEP coordination model.
Rewrite a vague issue into a clear coordination issue using location, systems, impact, and action.

lessons.topic: Installability as a BIM Thinking Habit
lessons.learning_outcome: Choose one routed element and describe the installation sequence in three simple steps.
Make a list of five MEP elements that usually require maintenance access.
Choose one mechanical equipment item and list the access zones needed around it.
Describe how you would compare a shaft on three different floors.
Choose one small ceiling grid and list all devices that need coordinated positions.

lessons.topic: Automation Should Reduce Repetition, Not Judgment
lessons.learning_outcome: Name one BIM task you would automate and one check you would perform after automation.

lessons.topic: Turning Model Review Into a Useful Report
lessons.learning_outcome: Design a five-column issue report template for beginner coordination review.
Rewrite one broad BIM question into a specific question with context and desired decision.

lessons.topic: Building a Personal BIM Learning Log
lessons.learning_outcome: Create a simple five-line BIM learning log template and fill it with one recent lesson.
Write your own 30-day MEP BIM improvement plan with three focus areas: tool skill, system understanding, and coordination judgment.

foundation.description: 8-track MEP BIM Foundation common to all disciplines

weekly_quiz: BIM Check Friday quiz sent every Friday (self-assessment)
